invariant_definition: Part, portion, or share. In Vedānta the term becomes a controlled way of saying that a dependent reality belongs to *brahman* or Bhagavān without immediately collapsing into strict identity. It therefore sits beside *prakāra*, *śakti*, *apṛthak-siddhi*, and the many forms of *bhedābheda*. A major scriptural driver is *Bhagavad-Gītā* 15.7, *mamaivāṃśo jīvaloke jīvabhūtaḥ sanātanaḥ*: the eternal *jīva* is 'my very own *aṃśa*.' Schools then ask what sort of partness this can be. Is the self a literal part of the Lord, a mode inseparable from Him, a dependent energy, or only figuratively called a part because of similarity or subordination? Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇavas distinguish *svāṃśa* manifestations of Bhagavān from *vibhinnāṃśa* selves; Bhāskara reads partness through real transformation; Advaita refuses literal partness because *brahman* is partless; Tattva-vāda (the school often labeled Dvaita) rejects it because the *jīva* is eternally distinct. 'Part' is thus never enough by itself; one must specify the metaphysics of the whole.

per_school.definition: Scriptural talk of the self as an *aṃśa* of the Lord is interpreted non-literally. Since *brahman* is partless, no real division can occur in it. Part-language therefore belongs to the empirical standpoint in which consciousness appears delimited by adjuncts, or to a pedagogical mode of speech marking dependence on *Īśvara*. The verse cannot establish real ontological segmentation in the absolute.

The self may be called an *aṃśa* of Brahman, but not as a detachable part. The intended sense is modal and inseparable: the *jīva* is a real mode of *brahman*, belonging to Him as body to soul. This preserves scriptural dependence language without making Brahman a composite substance divisible into pieces. The term is therefore subordinated to *apṛthak-siddhi* and *śarīra-śarīrī-bhāva*.

The self is a real *aṃśa* of *brahman* because the world and selves are real transformations of Brahman. The difference is genuine, yet the transformed entity is not outside its source. *Aṃśa* here therefore does real ontological work: it articulates difference within a still-real identity and rejects the Advaita move of treating difference as merely apparent.

The *jīva* is not a literal part of Viṣṇu. Part-language in scripture is explained through dependence, similarity, or belonging, never through actual ontological part-whole inclusion. Madhva's realism requires eternal difference between the Lord and the dependent self; if the self were truly a part of Viṣṇu, the distinction central to devotion and liberation would collapse.

The distinction between *svāṃśa* and *vibhinnāṃśa* is basic. Bhagavān's own plenary manifestations are *svāṃśa*; the innumerable *jīvas* are *vibhinnāṃśa*, separated portions belonging to the *taṭasthā-śakti*. This permits real belonging to Kṛṣṇa without erasing the soul's smallness, fallibility, and recoverable servitude. *Aṃśa* is therefore read through an ontology of *śakti* rather than through simple part-whole metaphysics.

translator_note: The lazy gloss is 'part.' In English that usually suggests a detachable piece cut from a larger whole. Most Sanskrit debates are precisely about refusing that picture while still preserving dependence, derivation, or manifestation. Translating *aṃśa* without noting whether the school means mode, power, transformation, or only figurative belonging imports a crude mereology the texts are arguing over.
